did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning, specifically Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) implemented via the TensorFlow library, to classify skin lesions (Melanoma vs. Melanocytic nevus) using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The study involved model training, hyperparameter tuning, and evaluation of classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model using the ISIC 2019 dataset to classify skin lesions. It includes specific sections on methodology (model training, hyperparameter tuning, loss functions), results (validation accuracy of 94.999%, precision/recall metrics), and evaluation, which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesion Classification using Machine Learning Algorithm for Differential Diagnosis
journal: 2022 2nd International Conference on Technological Advancements in Computational Sciences (ICTACS)
brief_summary: This study developed a machine learning-based decision support system to pre-classify skin lesions as either Melanoma or Melanocytic nevus using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The researchers implemented a simple Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with two hidden layers via the TensorFlow library to assist differential diagnosticians. The model was trained and evaluated on a subset of 360 images categorized into these two classes. The primary evaluation approach involved assessing classification metrics including precision, recall, and F1-score on a testing set. The key result was a validation accuracy of 94.999% and a weighted average F1-score of 0.93, demonstrating the model's effectiveness despite its simple topology. The authors conclude that this straightforward ANN approach offers a viable, resource-efficient alternative for hospital applications lacking advanced technical infrastructure.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes used in the binary classification experiment. The study explicitly excluded non-pigmented, subungual, ocular, and mucosal melanomas. It was classified using an ANN model on a subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into Melanoma or Melanocytic nevus
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the purpose is to 'pre-classify skin lesions as Melanoma or Melanocytic nevus'. The model is an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) trained on 360 images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The task is binary classification, as evidenced by the use of Categorical Cross-entropy loss function for two label classes, and evaluation metrics including precision, recall, and F1-score for classes 0 and 1. The reported validation accuracy is 94.999%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2019 challenge dataset, which includes training data from 2019 as well as previous years (2018 and 2017). It contains dermoscopic images for classification into nine diagnostic categories. The study utilized a subset of this dataset due to technical limitations.

use.training: The study states that 'The first 360 photos were taken due to technical limitations' and were used to train the Artificial Neural Network (ANN). Specific split counts for training and validation are not explicitly reported, though a validation accuracy of 94.999% is mentioned.
use.validation: Used for validation (accuracy reported as 94.999%), but specific count is not reported.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation. Table 1 and Table 2 indicate a testing set of 60 images (3 Melanoma, 57 Melanocytic nevus).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Evaluation was based on classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) and validation accuracy.

ISIC 2019 Dataset (Subset: Melanoma vs M.notes: The model achieved a validation accuracy of 94.999%. On the testing set, it correctly identified all Melanocytic nevi (Recall 1.00) but failed to identify any Melanomas (Recall 0.00). The weighted average F1-score was 0.93. The authors note that despite the simple topology and lower performance compared to CNNs, the model offers resource efficiency suitable for hospital applications lacking advanced technical infrastructure.

classification_or_output_head.activation: Softmax (implied by categorical cross-entropy and multi-class probability description)

preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of non-pigmented, subungual, ocular, and mucosal melanomas
Exclusion of images out of focus
Exclusion of images containing distracting artifacts such as obstructive gel bubbles
Exclusion of images containing terminal hairs
Exclusion of images containing clothing, jewelry, or tattoos
conclusions: A simple Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with two hidden layers was successfully trained to pre-classify skin lesions as Melanoma or Melanocytic nevus, achieving a validation accuracy of 94.999% and a weighted average F1-score of 0.93.

The proposed ANN model offers a viable, resource-efficient alternative for hospital applications, particularly in settings like average clinics in India that lack advanced technical infrastructure (such as high-end processors and GPUs) required for complex models like CNNs or RNNs.

The study demonstrates that machine learning can assist differential diagnosticians in making better decisions by reducing the stress associated with the diagnostic process, which typically takes 3-14 days for confirmation.

While the model showed high performance for Melanocytic nevus (Recall 1.00), it failed to identify any Melanoma cases in the testing set (Recall 0.00), highlighting limitations in detecting the minority class within the small, technically limited dataset of 360 images.
important_remarks: Critical clinical failure: The model achieved 0.00 recall for Melanoma (Class 0) on the testing set, meaning it failed to identify any malignant cases, rendering it unsafe for clinical use despite high overall accuracy.

Severe class imbalance: The testing set was heavily skewed towards Melanocytic nevus (57 samples) versus Melanoma (3 samples), which likely contributed to the model's inability to learn Melanoma features.

Extremely small dataset: The study used only 360 images total (300 training, 60 testing) due to 'technical limitations', which is insufficient for robust machine learning model development and generalization.
Lack of external validation: The model was evaluated solely on a subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset with no external validation cohort, limiting the generalizability of the results.

Simple architecture limitations: The use of a simple ANN with 32x32 pixel inputs and only 2 hidden layers lacks the feature extraction capabilities of CNNs, which are standard for dermatological image analysis.

No statistical significance testing: The paper reports classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) but does not provide p-values, confidence intervals, or statistical tests to validate the significance of the results.

Potential data leakage or split ambiguity: The paper states 'The first 360 photos were taken' but does not clearly specify the randomization or splitting methodology, raising concerns about how the training/testing split was constructed.

Limited scope: The study only addresses binary classification (Melanoma vs. Melanocytic nevus) and excludes other critical skin cancer types present in the ISIC 2019 dataset (e.g., Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma).
